text:
```json
{
  "analysis_summary": "Analyzed 5 RTL source files under the hackatdac21 access lock scope. The design implements a layered register-lock (reglk) and access-control (acct) mechanism for multiple cryptographic and system peripherals (AES, HMAC, SHA256, PKT/fuse key, boot ROM, etc.) on an OpenPiton/Ariane RISC-V SoC. Two permission-related security vulnerabilities were identified: (1) a copy-paste bug in reglk_wrapper.sv that causes write-lock bypass for one register slice, and (2) a JTAG-driven global unlock that resets all lock registers to the permissive state. A third potential concern is the default-open (all-ones) reset state of the access-control memory.",
  "findings": [
    {
      "finding_id": "F-01",
      "status": "confirmed_finding",
      "summary": "Copy-paste bug in reglk_wrapper write-side logic causes register lock bypass for reglk_mem[2] by referencing reglk_mem[3] instead of reglk_mem[2].",
      "vulnerability_category": "Access Control Bypass / Incorrect Register Lock Enforcement",
      "affected_locations": [
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 91,
          "line_end": 94,
          "module": "reglk_wrapper",
          "signal_or_register": "reglk_mem[2], reglk_mem[3]"
        }
      ],
      "evidence": [
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 89,
          "line_end": 99,
          "module": "reglk_wrapper",
          "object": "Always block write case",
          "evidence_type": "Source code - RTL logic",
          "description": "Lines 89-99 show the write-side case statement. Address 0 uses reglk_mem[0] correctly. Address 1 uses reglk_mem[1] correctly. Address 2 incorrectly uses 'reglk_mem[3]' as the hold value instead of 'reglk_mem[2]'. Addresses 3,4,5 correctly use their corresponding reglk_mem indices. The bug at address 2 means: reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;",
          "supports_claim": "When reglk_ctrl[1] is set (locked), writing to address 2 keeps the previous value of reglk_mem[3], NOT reglk_mem[2]. This means that if reglk_mem[2] and reglk_mem[3] hold different values, the lock on reglk_mem[2] is effectively broken because after a write to reglk_mem[3] changes its value, a subsequent locked write to address 2 will latch the (potentially attacker-controlled) value from reglk_mem[3]."
        },
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 48,
          "line_end": 48,
          "module": "reglk_wrapper",
          "object": "reglk_ctrl_o assignment",
          "evidence_type": "Source code - RTL logic",
          "description": "reglk_ctrl_o = {reglk_mem[5], reglk_mem[4], reglk_mem[3], reglk_mem[2], reglk_mem[1], reglk_mem[0]}; reglk_mem[2] is part of the output lock-control vector used by downstream peripherals.",
          "supports_claim": "Because reglk_mem[2] feeds into the lock control output, a corrupted value in reglk_mem[2] can unlock peripherals that depend on this lock bit."
        }
      ],
      "reasoning_summary": "The lock mechanism works by checking reglk_ctrl bits and conditionally blocking writes. For address offset 2, the code reads 'reglk_mem[3] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata' instead of 'reglk_mem[2] ? reglk_mem[2] : wdata'. This is a classic copy-paste error where the index was not updated from 3 to 2. When reglk_ctrl[1] is high (locked state), the register should hold its current value; but for reglk_mem[2] it holds the value of reglk_mem[3] instead, effectively bypassing the lock on reglk_mem[2] if reglk_mem[3] has been written with a permissive value.",
      "security_impact": "An attacker who can write to the reglk peripheral can manipulate reglk_mem[3] to a desired value, then write to address 2 (which is supposed to be locked). The 'locked' write to address 2 will adopt reglk_mem[3]'s value, allowing the attacker to change reglk_mem[2] despite the lock. Since reglk_mem[2] is part of the 112-bit lock control vector distributed to other peripherals, this can unlock protected registers in peripherals such as HMAC, PKT, AES, etc.",
      "confidence": "high",
      "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The exact mapping of reglk_mem[2] lock bits to specific peripheral protections is inferred from the reglk_ctrl_o concatenation. The full riscv_peripherals.sv wiring could not be completely read due to file size, so the exact peripherals affected by reglk_mem[2] are not 100% confirmed.",
      "recommended_follow_up": [
        "Fix line 93: change 'reglk_mem[3]' to 'reglk_mem[2]' in the ternary expression.",
        "Audit all other case entries in reglk_wrapper, acct_wrapper, hmac_wrapper, and pkt_wrapper for similar copy-paste errors.",
        "Consider adding formal assertions to verify that each locked register holds its value when its corresponding lock bit is set."
      ]
    },
    {
      "finding_id": "F-02",
      "status": "confirmed_finding",
      "summary": "JTAG unlock signal (jtag_unlock) globally resets ALL register-lock memory to zero, disabling all lock protections across the entire SoC.",
      "vulnerability_category": "Insufficient Access Control / JTAG-Induced Global Unlock",
      "affected_locations": [
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 78,
          "line_end": 84,
          "module": "reglk_wrapper",
          "signal_or_register": "jtag_unlock, reglk_mem[0:5]"
        }
      ],
      "evidence": [
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 78,
          "line_end": 84,
          "module": "reglk_wrapper",
          "object": "Reset condition for reglk_mem",
          "evidence_type": "Source code - RTL logic",
          "description": "if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)) begin for (j=0; j < 6; j=j+1) begin reglk_mem[j] <= 'h0; end end. When jtag_unlock is asserted, the condition evaluates to true (reset), and all six 32-bit reglk_mem registers are cleared to zero.",
          "supports_claim": "Zero in reglk_mem means 'unlocked' for all lock bits. A single JTAG unlock signal disables all register-lock protections globally."
        },
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 15,
          "line_end": 27,
          "module": "reglk_wrapper",
          "object": "Module ports",
          "evidence_type": "Source code - module declaration",
          "description": "jtag_unlock is a top-level input to reglk_wrapper, sourced from the JTAG/debug infrastructure.",
          "supports_claim": "The jtag_unlock signal is an externally controllable input, meaning JTAG access can trigger the global unlock."
        }
      ],
      "reasoning_summary": "The reset condition `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)` causes reglk_mem to reset to all zeros whenever jtag_unlock is high. All zeros in reglk_mem means no register is locked. The lock bits from reglk_mem are distributed to all peripherals (HMAC, ACCT, PKT, AES, SHA256, RNG, RSA, etc.) via reglk_ctrl_o. Asserting jtag_unlock therefore removes all write protections across the entire SoC.",
      "security_impact": "If an attacker gains JTAG access (e.g., via physical access, debug port exploitation, or a compromised debug agent), they can assert jtag_unlock and instantly disable all register locks. This allows arbitrary read/write access to cryptographic keys (HMAC key, AES keys), fuse-derived private keys (via PKT), access control registers, and all other locked peripherals. This represents a single-point-of-failure for the entire hardware security architecture.",
      "confidence": "high",
      "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The conditions under which jtag_unlock can be asserted in the full SoC are not fully visible from these source files alone. The JTAG/debug module access control (e.g., whether it requires authentication) is not part of this scope.",
      "recommended_follow_up": [
        "Consider requiring an authenticated unlock sequence (e.g., a password or cryptographic challenge-response) before jtag_unlock takes effect.",
        "Alternatively, make jtag_unlock only clear a subset of lock bits (e.g., only debug-related locks) rather than all locks.",
        "Add a tamper-evident or irrevocable lock bit that prevents jtag_unlock from clearing certain high-security lock registers."
      ]
    },
    {
      "finding_id": "F-03",
      "status": "potential_warning",
      "summary": "Access control memory (acct_mem) resets to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), defaulting to permissive access for all peripherals.",
      "vulnerability_category": "Insecure Default Configuration / Default-Open Access Control",
      "affected_locations": [
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 76,
          "line_end": 81,
          "module": "acct_wrapper",
          "signal_or_register": "acct_mem"
        }
      ],
      "evidence": [
        {
          "file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv",
          "line_start": 76,
          "line_end": 81,
          "module": "acct_wrapper",
          "object": "Reset block for acct_mem",
          "evidence_type": "Source code - RTL logic",
          "description": "if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)) begin for (j=0; j < AcCt_MEM_SIZE; j=j+1) begin acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff; end end. All access control registers reset to 0xFFFFFFFF (all access permitted).",
          "supports_claim": "On reset, all access control bits default to 1 (permissive), meaning every peripheral is accessible until software explicitly locks them down."
        }
      ],
      "reasoning_summary": "The principle of 'secure by default' or 'fail-secure' dictates that security mechanisms should start in the most restrictive state and software should explicitly grant access. Here, acct_mem resets to all-ones, granting full access to all peripherals. Between reset and the point where trusted software configures the access control registers, there is a window where untrusted code could access protected peripherals.",
      "security_impact": "During the boot window before access control is configured, any code running on the processor (including early boot firmware or potentially malicious code that gains control early) has unrestricted access to all cryptographic and security peripherals including AES, HMAC keys, PKT fuse key table, and RNG.",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The processor's boot flow, PMP configuration timing, and whether any other mechanism (e.g., boot ROM locking) prevents early access during this window is not visible in this scope. The acct control signals are gated by priv_lvl_i in riscv_peripherals.sv, but the priv_lvl_i routing and boot privilege level are not fully analyzed.",
      "recommended_follow_up": [
        "Consider resetting acct_mem to all-zeros (default deny) instead of all-ones.",
        "Ensure that the boot ROM sets up access control before any untrusted code can execute.",
        "Verify that the privilege level (priv_lvl_i) is forced to Machine-mode during early boot."
      ]
    }
  ],
  "no_finding_reason": "N/A - findings were identified.",
  "global_uncertainty": "Only 5 source files were available for analysis (4 wrappers + 1 integration file). The underlying peripheral implementations (AES, HMAC core, PKT/fuse controller, SHA256 core), the AXI-lite bridge modules, and the processor's PMP/privilege-level enforcement logic are outside this scope. The full riscv_peripherals.sv file was truncated in tool output, so some wiring details of lock bit routing to specific peripherals could not be confirmed. The actual security of the system depends on factors beyond this RTL scope (boot flow, JTAG authentication, physical security, etc.)."
}
```
